title: T2: Use case tests with fake repositories (no DB/network)
description:
## What
Write unit tests for use cases using fake (in-memory) repositories and providers. These tests validate workflow logic without any infrastructure dependencies.

## Files to Create
- backend/tests/unit/use_cases/conftest.py — Shared fake implementations:
  - FakeScanRepository (in-memory dict)
  - FakeScanResultRepository (in-memory list)
  - FakeUniverseRepository (returns configured symbol lists)
  - FakeStockDataProvider (returns deterministic StockData)
  - FakeProgressSink (collects events in a list)
  - FakeCancellationToken (configurable)

- backend/tests/unit/use_cases/test_create_scan.py
  - Happy path: creates scan with expected fields
  - Idempotency: same key returns existing scan
  - Universe resolution called correctly

- backend/tests/unit/use_cases/test_run_bulk_scan.py
  - Happy path: processes all symbols, writes results
  - Cancellation: stops between chunks, status updated
  - Checkpointing: resume skips already-processed symbols
  - Error handling: failed symbols don't crash the run

- backend/tests/unit/use_cases/test_get_scan_results.py
  - Pagination: correct page size and total count
  - Filtering: FilterSpec applied correctly
  - Sorting: SortSpec applied correctly

## Why Fake Repos
Fake repos (not mocks) test actual behavior. A FakeScanRepository stores data in a dict and returns it — so we verify the use case calls the right methods with the right data, not just that it 'called save once'.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All use case happy paths tested
- Error and edge cases covered
- Tests run in <2 seconds total (no IO)
- No mocking of domain logic — only infrastructure ports are faked

## Depends On
- C1 (RunBulkScanUseCase must exist)
close_reason: All 3 use case test files consolidated under tests/unit/use_cases/ with shared conftest.py fakes. 37 tests pass. Backward-compat re-exports in scanning_fakes.py.

title: F1: Create BuildDailyFeatureSnapshotUseCase
description:
## What
Implement the core offline pipeline use case that builds a complete feature snapshot for a trading day.

## Files to Create
- backend/app/use_cases/feature_store/build_daily_snapshot.py

## Algorithm (from v3 plan Phase F)
1. Determine as_of_date trading day (domain time policy — skip weekends/holidays)
2. FeatureRunRepository.start_run(...) with correlation_id
3. Resolve universe of active symbols via UniverseRepository (likely 'ALL' or configured set)
4. Persist resolved universe: FeatureStoreRepository.save_run_universe_symbols(run_id, symbols)
5. Merge data requirements from all configured screeners
6. Call StockDataProvider.prepare_bulk(symbols, requirements, as_of_date, allow_partial=True)
7. For each symbol with data:
   - Run orchestrator (domain, pure) with pre-fetched data
   - Collect ScanResultItemDomain
8. FeatureStoreRepository.upsert_snapshot_rows(run_id, rows)
9. Run DQ checks (domain/feature_store/quality.py):
   - Row count vs expected universe size
   - Null rate on composite_score
   - Score distribution sanity
10. If DQ fails → mark_quarantined(run_id, dq_report) — keep data for inspection
11. If DQ passes → publish_atomically(run_id) — atomic pointer update
12. Emit completion ProgressEvent with stats

## Error Handling
- allow_partial=True means partial upstream failures don't crash the whole run
- Failed symbols logged in warnings_json
- Run is still publishable if DQ passes despite partial failures (configurable threshold)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Can build and publish a snapshot for a given date
- Quarantines on DQ failure (doesn't publish bad data)
- Handles partial upstream failures gracefully
- Idempotent: re-running for same date creates a new run (doesn't corrupt existing)
- Unit-testable with fake repos and fake StockDataProvider

## Depends On
- E3 (Feature Store repositories)
- B3 (port-based orchestrator)
- C1 (pattern for use case structure)
close_reason: Implemented BuildDailyFeatureSnapshotUseCase with 22 passing tests, DQ checks, publish/quarantine, cancellation, chunked scanning, and progress events

title: Z2: Consolidate Pydantic schemas after migration
description:
## What
During the strangler migration, some Pydantic schemas may have been duplicated (inline in router vs separate files). Consolidate them into a clean schema layer.

## Current State
The scan router (backend/app/api/v1/scans.py) has many schemas defined inline (~100+ lines of Pydantic models at the top of the file). During the refactor, domain types were added alongside these.

## Target State
- backend/app/schemas/scanning.py — All scan-related request/response schemas
- backend/app/schemas/feature_store.py — Feature store API schemas
- Remove inline schema definitions from routers
- Clear mapping: domain types ↔ API schemas (explicit conversion functions)

## Consolidation Rules
- Request schemas: Pydantic models that validate API input → kept in schemas/
- Response schemas: Pydantic models that format API output → kept in schemas/
- Domain types: dataclasses in domain/ — NOT Pydantic (no validation dependencies)
- Conversion: explicit to_domain() and from_domain() methods on schemas

## Acceptance Criteria
- No inline Pydantic models in router files
- Clear schema files organized by feature area
- Domain types remain pure (no Pydantic dependency)
- All API contracts unchanged (same JSON shape)
- OpenAPI docs still generate correctly

## Depends On
- Z1 (legacy paths removed, so we know what's actually used)
close_reason: Extracted 34 inline Pydantic schemas from 6 routers into 7 schema files, added from_domain() classmethods, updated 2 test files. 225 tests pass.

title: T4: Golden scan parity test — legacy vs feature store comparison
description:
## What
Create a golden test that verifies feature store results match legacy scan results for a fixed set of tickers. This is the ultimate validation that the refactor preserved behavior.

## Files to Create
- backend/tests/parity/test_scan_parity.py
- backend/tests/parity/fixtures/ — Recorded test data

## Test Design
1. Pick 20 stable large-cap tickers (AAPL, MSFT, GOOGL, etc.)
2. Record legacy scan results for these tickers at a fixed as_of_date
3. Build a feature store snapshot for the same date with same config
4. Compare field by field:
   - composite_score: within 0.01 tolerance (floating point)
   - overall_rating: exact match
   - passes_count: exact match
   - Per-screener scores: within 0.01 tolerance
5. Compare filtered/sorted result ordering for common filter sets

## Why Tolerance
Floating point arithmetic may differ slightly between compute-per-request and batch paths due to ordering of operations. 0.01 tolerance prevents false failures while catching real regressions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- 20-ticker parity test passes
- Composite scores match within tolerance
- Ratings match exactly
- Filter/sort ordering stable
- Test is reproducible (uses recorded fixture data, not live API)

## Depends On
- G2 (dual-source query must work to compare both paths)
close_reason: All acceptance criteria met: 21-ticker golden parity tests implemented in commit f65bc4e5, covering field-by-field comparison with 0.01 tolerance, exact rating matches, filter/sort ordering, pagination, and schema alignment — all using deterministic fixture data against in-memory SQLite.

title: B1: Extract composite score + rating policies to domain layer
description:
## What
Extract pure scoring and rating logic from ScanOrchestrator into domain modules. These become pure functions with zero IO dependencies.

## Current State
- backend/app/scanners/scan_orchestrator.py contains:
  - _calculate_composite_score() (~line 350-400): weighted_average, maximum, minimum methods
  - _calculate_overall_rating() (~line 400-450): threshold-based rating from composite score

## Files to Create
- backend/app/domain/scanning/composite.py — CompositeScorer class
  - Methods: weighted_average, maximum, minimum
  - Input: dict of screener scores + weights
  - Output: float composite score
  - Pure function, no IO

- backend/app/domain/scanning/rating.py — RatingCalculator class
  - Input: composite score, pass rates, thresholds config
  - Output: integer rating (1-5 scale)
  - Must match EXACT current thresholds from scan_orchestrator.py
  - Pure function, no IO

## Why This Matters
These are the core business rules of the screener. By extracting them to domain/, they become:
1. Unit-testable without mocking anything
2. Reusable by both online scan and offline feature store
3. Protected from accidental IO coupling

## Acceptance Criteria
- CompositeScorer produces identical results to current _calculate_composite_score
- RatingCalculator produces identical results to current _calculate_overall_rating
- Both have zero imports outside domain/ and stdlib
- Boundary test (from A1) passes
- Existing scan results unchanged (integration verify)

## Depends On
- A1 (domain/scanning/ package must exist)
close_reason: Created domain/scanning/scoring.py with calculate_composite_score and calculate_overall_rating pure functions. Boundary tests pass, smoke tests verify identical behavior to orchestrator.

title: C1: Create RunBulkScanUseCase with progress, cancellation, checkpointing
description:
## What
Create the use case that contains all scan execution business logic, extracted from the current Celery task file.

## Current State (backend/app/tasks/scan_tasks.py, ~1120 lines)
The run_bulk_scan Celery task currently handles:
- Loading Scan record from DB
- Resolving universe symbols
- Updating scan status/progress in DB
- Running batch orchestration (chunked)
- Persisting scan results
- Cleanup and retention policies
All mixed with Celery-specific state updates and error handling.

## Files to Create
- backend/app/use_cases/scanning/run_bulk_scan.py — RunBulkScanUseCase class

## RunBulkScanUseCase.execute(scan_id, correlation_id, cancellation_token) should:
1. Load Scan record via ScanRepository port
2. Resolve universe symbols via UniverseRepository port
3. Update scan status to 'running' via ScanRepository
4. Emit ProgressEvents via ProgressSink port (append-only timeline)
5. Process symbols in chunks:
   a. Check cancellation_token between chunks
   b. Save checkpoint after each chunk (for resumability)
   c. Call orchestrator (domain) with pre-fetched data
6. Persist results via ScanResultRepository port
7. Update scan status to 'completed' with final stats
8. Handle errors: update status to 'failed' with error details

## Ports Required (add to domain/scanning/ports.py)
- ScanRepository: create, get, update_status
- ScanResultRepository: bulk_upsert, query_results
- UniverseRepository: resolve_symbols
- ProgressSink: emit(ProgressEvent)
- CancellationToken: is_cancelled() -> bool

## Key Design Principle
The use case orchestrates the WORKFLOW but contains no IO code. All IO goes through ports. This means:
- Unit-testable with fake repos (no DB, no Redis, no Celery)
- Retry-safe: checkpointing ensures no duplicate work on resume
- Celery-agnostic: could be called from CLI, API, or test

## Acceptance Criteria
- Use case handles full scan lifecycle (create → running → completed/failed)
- Progress events emitted at meaningful milestones
- Cancellation checked between chunks
- Checkpoint save/restore prevents duplicate processing on retry
- Zero Celery imports in this file
- Unit test with fake repos covers happy path + cancellation + resume

## Depends On
- B3 (port-based orchestrator)
- A3 (CreateScanUseCase pattern established)

## What

## Files to Modify

  def execute(scan_id, filter_spec, sort_spec, paging_spec):
    scan = scan_repo.get(scan_id)
    if scan.feature_run_id:
      # Query feature store
      return feature_store_repo.query_run(scan.feature_run_id, filter_spec, sort_spec, paging_spec)
    else:
      # Legacy path: query scan_results table
      return scan_result_repo.query_results(scan_id, filter_spec, sort_spec, paging_spec)

- backend/app/infra/query/feature_store_query_sqlalchemy.py — Query builder for stock_feature_daily table

  - JSON extraction for details_json fields

## Key Consideration
Both paths must return the same domain type (Page[ScanResultItemDomain]) so the router doesn't know or care about the source. This is the Liskov Substitution Principle in action.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Bound scans query feature store
- Unbound scans query legacy scan_results
- Same response format regardless of source
- No breaking API changes
- Performance: feature store queries should be faster (pre-indexed, no JSON parsing in hot path)

- D1 (GetScanResultsUseCase must exist)

title: A2: Add wiring/bootstrap.py dependency injection graph
description:
## What
Create the dependency injection bootstrap module that wires together repositories, providers, and use cases. This is the single place where concrete implementations are bound to abstract ports.

## Files to Create

## Implementation Details
bootstrap.py should provide:
- get_uow() -> UnitOfWork factory (wraps SessionLocal)
- get_scan_repo() -> ScanRepository (SQLAlchemy impl)
- get_scan_result_repo() -> ScanResultRepository
- get_universe_repo() -> UniverseRepository
- get_stock_data_provider() -> StockDataProvider (DataPreparationLayer adapter)
- get_create_scan_use_case() -> CreateScanUseCase (wired with repos)

Use FastAPI's Depends() integration pattern so routers can inject use cases.

## Also Create
- backend/app/infra/db/uow.py — UnitOfWork class wrapping SQLAlchemy session lifecycle

## Why
Centralizing wiring prevents each module from importing concrete implementations directly. This is what makes the port/adapter pattern actually work — you swap implementations in ONE place.

## Acceptance Criteria
- bootstrap.py can instantiate all registered use cases
- UnitOfWork properly manages session lifecycle (commit/rollback)
- FastAPI Depends() integration works for router injection
- No changes to existing behavior

## Depends On
- A1 (package structure must exist)

DQ checks as pure domain functions means they can be unit-tested trivially and reused across different run types (daily, backfill, manual).

title: E1: Create Feature Store SQLAlchemy models + migration
description:
## What
Create the database schema for the Feature Store — the core persistence layer for pre-computed screening results.

## Tables to Create (see v3 plan Appendix A)

### feature_runs
- id (Integer PK, autoincrement)
- as_of_date (Date, indexed)
- run_type (Text) — daily_snapshot / backfill / manual
- status (Text) — running / completed / failed / quarantined / published
- created_at, completed_at, published_at (DateTime)
- code_version (Text) — git SHA or app version
- universe_hash (Text) — hash of resolved symbol list for reproducibility
- input_hash (Text) — hash of config/criteria for cache-busting
- correlation_id (Text, indexed) — traces across API→Celery→DB
- stats_json (JSON) — row counts, timing, coverage stats
- warnings_json (JSON) — DQ warnings, partial failures

### feature_run_universe_symbols
- run_id (FK → feature_runs.id, indexed)
- symbol (Text, indexed)
- PRIMARY KEY (run_id, symbol)

### stock_feature_daily
- run_id (FK → feature_runs.id, indexed)
- symbol (Text, indexed)
- as_of_date (Date, indexed) — denormalized for query convenience
- composite_score (Real, indexed)
- overall_rating (Integer, indexed)
- passes_count (Integer)
- details_json (JSON) — per-screener breakdown
- PRIMARY KEY (run_id, symbol)

### feature_run_pointers
- key (Text PK) — e.g. 'latest_published'
- run_id (FK → feature_runs.id)
- updated_at (DateTime)

## Indexes (minimum for query-per-request)
- stock_feature_daily(run_id, composite_score DESC)
- stock_feature_daily(run_id, overall_rating DESC)
- stock_feature_daily(run_id, symbol)

## Files to Create
- backend/app/infra/db/models/feature_store.py — SQLAlchemy model classes
- backend/migrations/add_feature_store_tables.py — Migration script

## Acceptance Criteria
- All tables created successfully on fresh DB
- Migration runs cleanly on existing production DB
- Foreign key constraints enforced
- Indexes verified with EXPLAIN on common queries

## Depends On
- A2 (infra/db structure must exist)

title: F3: Implement DQ checks + quarantine semantics for feature runs
description:
## What
Implement the data quality validation pipeline that gates publishing of feature runs. Bad data should be quarantined, not published.

## Components

### Already Created (E2)
- domain/feature_store/quality.py — Pure DQ check functions

### To Create
- backend/app/use_cases/feature_store/publish_run.py — PublishFeatureRunUseCase
  - Input: run_id, dq_overrides (optional, for manual override)
  - Logic:
    1. Load run from FeatureRunRepository
    2. Load snapshot stats (row count, null rates, score distribution)
    3. Run DQ checks (domain functions)
    4. If all pass → publish_atomically(run_id)
    5. If any fail → mark_quarantined(run_id, dq_report)
    6. Return publish result with DQ report

### DQ Checks to Implement
1. Row count check: actual rows >= 90% of universe size
2. Null rate check: composite_score null rate < 5%
3. Score distribution: mean composite score within expected range
4. Rating distribution: at least some stocks in each rating bucket (sanity)
5. Symbol coverage: check no unexpected symbols missing vs universe

## Quarantine Behavior
- Quarantined runs are NOT published (latest pointer unchanged)
- Quarantined runs are kept in DB for inspection/debugging
- Admin can manually publish a quarantined run via override flag
- DQ report stored in warnings_json for visibility

## Acceptance Criteria
- DQ checks run automatically during snapshot build (F1 calls this)
- Quarantined runs visible but not served to API consumers
- Manual publish override works for admin use cases
- Unit tests cover each DQ check independently

## Depends On
- F1 (called during snapshot build)

- backend/tests/load/README.md — operator docs
- backend/pytest.ini — added 'load' marker
- Makefile — added gate-6-load and load-baseline-update targets

DESIGN DECISIONS (per 2026-04-14 conversation):
1. Hybrid yfinance: deterministic simulator for CI, LOAD_TEST_LIVE=1 flag stub for occasional out-of-band evidence-pack runs (currently skipped — needs additional safeguards before live runs).
2. Fixed synthetic universe (200/100/100/80) sized to 4-5 batches per market — exercises per-market batch-size differences (US 50, HK/JP 25, TW 20) without dragging out runtime.
3. All three measurement classes (wall-clock + 429 + tail-latency + memory/CPU) recorded in JSON; only wall-clock and 429 count gate CI.
4. New gate-6-load target (not gate-4 extension) — load tests are inherently slower/side-effect-heavy and shouldn't run per-PR. Recommended schedule: nightly + manual when touching rate-budget code.
5. Baselines committed to repo as authoritative contract; current_run.json is ephemeral. Operators run `make load-baseline-update` to regenerate after intentional perf changes.
6. Comparison report only — no active recommender. The harness emits a clear regression diff; humans decide whether to re-tune constants.
7. Single realistic scenario (4 markets parallel during Sunday weekly window). Stress scenarios are 9.4 fault-injection territory.
8. Standalone tests/load/ directory — load tests have very different semantics from query-perf tests in tests/performance/.

KEY HARNESS PROPERTIES:
- 1.4 second runtime per execution (vs initial 5-minute attempt before time.sleep no-op patching).
- Thread-safe: single MultiMarketSimulator routes per-call by symbol prefix, eliminating the per-thread patch.object race.
- time.sleep patched no-op in bulk_data_fetcher + rate_limiter so backoff branches execute their logic without literal wait.
- Same seed → bit-identical output (verified twice in row); different seed → triggers regression detection (verified with LOAD_TEST_SEED=99).
- Reset fixture clears only the harness's known Redis prefixes (ratelimit:* / data_fetch_job_lock* / cache:warmup:*) — never touches arbitrary application keys.
- per-market batch counts in baseline match universe_size/batch_size: US 200/50=4, HK 100/25=4, JP/TW have +1 retry batches due to seeded 429 injections — visible verification that 9.2's per-market batch-size policy is actually being applied to the hot path.

DEFERRED (out of scope for 9.3):
- Live-yfinance runner with IP rate limiting and longer baselines.
- Active recommender that suggests new settings values from measurements.
- Per-market memory/CPU breakdown (currently single process-level sample).
- GitHub Actions workflow YAML for the nightly schedule (operator can wire `make gate-6-load` into their CI of choice).

Tests: 1/1 in load suite passes; 118/118 in 9.x unit suite still green; regression gating verified by intentional seed change.

title: T4 Fault-injection tests for failure isolation
description: Inject provider/queue failures and verify non-affected markets continue operating.
notes:
Fault tests validate the operational assumption that failures remain local and recovery does not require global throughput pauses.

Implementation complete 2026-04-15:

NEW FILES:
- backend/tests/load/fault_injection.py — three injectors:
  - inject_provider_exhaustion: forces victim market to 100% 429
  - inject_provider_hard_failure: forces victim market to raise non-429 exceptions
  - hold_market_lock: context manager that externally acquires data_fetch_job_lock:<victim>
- backend/tests/load/test_failure_isolation.py — three curated scenarios mapping each fault to the specific 9.1/9.2 isolation mechanism it exercises
- backend/tests/load/harness.py — extracted from test_per_market_load.py: no_sleep, make_simulator_module, read_429_counter, run_one_market, run_parallel_refresh

MODIFIED:
- backend/tests/load/test_per_market_load.py — refactored to use the shared harness module; functionally equivalent
- backend/tests/load/measurement.py — gate metric switched from wall_clock_s (system-noisy, ±30% across runs) to yfinance_calls (deterministic from seed). 429 count threshold unchanged at +50%. Wall-clock + tail-latency remain in the snapshot for trend analysis but no longer gate CI.
- backend/tests/load/baselines/per_market_load.json — regenerated with stabilized harness
- backend/tests/load/README.md — added Fault Isolation section + scenario→mechanism table
- Makefile — added gate-7-chaos target (separate from gate-6-load)

DESIGN DECISIONS (per 2026-04-15 conversation):
1. Faults: provider exhaustion (a), provider hard failure (b), lock stuck (d). Slow-loris (c), Redis outage (e), backoff exhaustion (f) deferred — those test mechanisms not yet observed to be flaky.
2. Reused tests/load/ infrastructure rather than new tests/chaos/ directory; the multi-market simulator + Redis reset fixture + parallel runner are identical concerns.
3. Curated 3 scenarios (not parameterized matrix); each test's docstring documents exactly which 9.1/9.2 isolation mechanism is being validated.
4. New gate-7-chaos target separate from gate-6-load — same nightly cadence but distinct exit signal lets operators see "perf regression" vs "isolation regression" independently.
5. Asserts all 4 isolation guarantees per non-affected market: symbol count match, transient-failure count match (NOT zero — healthy baseline already has built-in failures), 429 counter zero, yfinance_calls match. All four are gated.
6. Documentation lives in tests/load/README.md as an extended section — no separate runbook file.

GATING METRIC CHANGE (caught by 4-run stability check):
The original 9.3 design gated on wall_clock_s with +20% threshold. Real measurement showed ±30% variance between identical runs (system noise dominates the deterministic 1.4s test). Changed to gate on yfinance_calls (purely deterministic from RNG seed + pipeline logic — any change is a real shift in fetcher batch behavior). This is a more honest signal: the harness exists to detect changes in PIPELINE BEHAVIOR (call counts, rate-limit counts), not WALL-CLOCK MEASUREMENT.

VERIFICATION:
- 3 chaos tests pass: provider_exhaustion, provider_hard_failure, lock_stuck.
- 1 load test still passes after refactor.
- Stability: 3 consecutive identical runs all pass.
- Unit suite: 105/105 still pass.

WHAT 9.4 SHIPS AGAINST AC ("Isolation guarantees validated and documented"):
- Validated: 3 chaos scenarios assert 4 isolation guarantees each = 12 distinct assertions per CI run, covering symbol completeness, failure containment, rate-key isolation, and deterministic batch behavior across 3 distinct fault types.
- Documented: README scenario→mechanism mapping table + each test's docstring names the specific 9.1/9.2 mechanism it exercises.

title: A3: Refactor POST /scans to use CreateScanUseCase
description:
## What
Refactor the POST /api/v1/scans endpoint to use CreateScanUseCase, proving the layered architecture works end-to-end.

## Current State (backend/app/api/v1/scans.py)
The create_scan() router function (~100 lines) currently:
1. Parses request body (universe definition, screener config)
2. Resolves universe symbols via universe_resolver
3. Creates Scan DB record directly
4. Dispatches Celery task
All in one function — mixing transport, business logic, and IO.

## Target State
Router create_scan() becomes:
1. Parse/validate request (FastAPI layer)
2. Call CreateScanUseCase.execute(config) via Depends()
3. Return response

## Files to Create
- backend/app/use_cases/scanning/create_scan.py — CreateScanUseCase class
- backend/app/domain/scanning/ports.py — ScanRepository, UniverseRepository protocols (initial)

## Files to Modify
- backend/app/api/v1/scans.py — Slim down create_scan to call use case

## Additional Requirements
- Add idempotency_key support: repeated POST with same key returns existing scan
- Celery task dispatch only after DB commit (use on_commit hook or outbox pattern)
- CreateScanUseCase should be unit-testable with fake repos

## Acceptance Criteria
- Same API input/output (no breaking changes)
- Router function is <20 lines
- Unit test for CreateScanUseCase with fake repos
- Idempotent: same idempotency_key returns same scan_id

## Depends On
- A2 (wiring/bootstrap must exist)

title: T6: Query performance guardrails — prevent N+1 regressions
description:
## What
Add lightweight performance benchmarks that catch query regressions (especially N+1 queries) in the feature store path.

## Files to Create
- backend/tests/performance/test_query_performance.py

## Test Design
1. Seed a minimal feature store table (~500 rows, 1 run)
2. Run common query patterns:
   - Paginated results (page 1 of 20)
   - Filtered by rating >= 3
   - Sorted by composite_score DESC
   - Filter + sort combined
3. Assert for each query:
   - Query count <= expected (usually 2: count + select)
   - Execution time < budget (e.g., 100ms for SQLite)
4. Compare against legacy scan_results queries for same patterns

## Implementation
- Use SQLAlchemy event listeners to count queries
- Use pytest fixtures to seed consistent test data
- Budgets should be generous (not flaky) but catch order-of-magnitude regressions

## Acceptance Criteria
- No N+1 queries in any tested pattern
- Query count assertions prevent regression
- Timing budgets catch major performance issues
- Tests run in <10 seconds (including DB setup)

## Depends On
- G2 (feature store query path must exist)
close_reason: Implemented: 21 query perf tests (12 query count, 3 invariance, 6 timing), shared count_queries helper, performance marker

title: D1: Create GetScanResultsUseCase — paginated, filtered, sorted query
description:
## What
Extract the scan results query logic from the router into a proper use case. This is the most complex read endpoint — it handles filtering, sorting, and pagination.

## Current State (backend/app/api/v1/scans.py)
The get_scan_results endpoint (~350 lines of filter construction) directly:
- Parses 20+ filter parameters from query string
- Builds SQLAlchemy queries with JSON extraction for details fields
- Handles sorting on both regular columns and JSON-extracted fields
- Implements pagination
- Maps DB rows to response format

## Files to Create
- backend/app/use_cases/scanning/get_scan_results.py — GetScanResultsUseCase
  - Input: scan_id, FilterSpec, SortSpec, PagingSpec (domain types from B2)
  - Output: Page[ScanResultItemDomain] with total count
  - Uses ScanResultRepository.query_results() port

- backend/app/infra/query/scan_query_sqlalchemy.py — SQLAlchemy query builder
  - Translates FilterSpec → SQLAlchemy WHERE clauses
  - Translates SortSpec → ORDER BY
  - Handles JSON field extraction for details-based filters
  - Implements efficient COUNT + SELECT in single query

## Why This Architecture
By putting the query builder in infra/ and the use case in use_cases/, we can:
- Test the use case with a fake repo (returns canned data)
- Test the query builder against a real SQLite DB in integration tests
- Swap the query builder for a Postgres-specific one later without touching use case

## Acceptance Criteria
- Use case returns same results as current endpoint for all filter combinations
- Router becomes thin: parse params → build specs → call use case → return response
- FilterSpec supports all current filter fields
- Pagination returns correct total counts
- No N+1 queries

## Depends On
- A2 (wiring/bootstrap for injection)
close_reason: Implemented GetScanResultsUseCase with query builder, port extension, wiring, and 83 passing unit tests

title: G1: Add Scan.feature_run_id binding
description:
## What
Add a feature_run_id column to the Scan model so scans can be bound to a specific Feature Store run. This is the bridge between the online scan API and the offline feature store.

## Files to Modify
- backend/app/models/scan.py (or wherever Scan model lives) — Add:
  - feature_run_id (Integer, FK → feature_runs.id, nullable=True)
  - Nullable because legacy scans predate the feature store

- backend/app/use_cases/scanning/create_scan.py — Update CreateScanUseCase:
  - On scan creation, look up latest published feature run
  - If available, bind scan.feature_run_id = latest_run.id
  - If no published run exists, leave null (legacy behavior)

## Migration
- backend/migrations/add_scan_feature_run_id.py — Add column with ALTER TABLE

## Why This Design
Binding at creation time (not query time) ensures:
1. Scan results are reproducible — they always reference the same snapshot
2. No 'drift' if a new snapshot publishes while user is viewing results
3. Clear audit trail of which data backed each scan

## Acceptance Criteria
- New scans get feature_run_id when a published run exists
- Legacy scans (null feature_run_id) continue working unchanged
- Migration runs cleanly on existing DB
- CreateScanUseCase updated with binding logic

## Depends On
- E3 (Feature Store repos must exist for lookup)
- A3 (CreateScanUseCase must exist)

title: E3: Create Feature Store repositories — FeatureRunRepo, FeatureStoreRepo
description:
## What
Implement the repository layer for Feature Store with atomic publish semantics.

## Files to Create

### Domain ports (protocols)
- backend/app/domain/feature_store/ports.py:
  - FeatureRunRepository Protocol:
    - start_run(as_of_date, run_type, code_version, universe_hash, input_hash, correlation_id) -> FeatureRunDomain
    - mark_completed(run_id, stats, warnings)
    - mark_quarantined(run_id, dq_report)
    - publish_atomically(run_id) — MUST update status + pointer in same transaction
    - get_latest_published() -> FeatureRunDomain | None
    - get_run(run_id) -> FeatureRunDomain

  - FeatureStoreRepository Protocol:
    - upsert_snapshot_rows(run_id, rows: list[FeatureRowWrite])
    - save_run_universe_symbols(run_id, symbols: list[str])
    - query_latest(spec: FilterSpec, sort: SortSpec, paging: PagingSpec) -> Page[FeatureRow]
    - query_run(run_id, spec, sort, paging) -> Page[FeatureRow]

### Infra implementations
- backend/app/infra/db/repositories/feature_run_repo_sqlalchemy.py
- backend/app/infra/db/repositories/feature_store_repo_sqlalchemy.py

## Critical: Atomic Publish
publish_atomically() must in a SINGLE transaction:
1. Set feature_runs.status = 'published', published_at = now
2. Update feature_run_pointers where key='latest_published' → run_id
This prevents readers from seeing a mixed state.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Repositories implement all port methods
- Atomic publish verified: no partial state observable
- Integration test with SQLite confirms CRUD operations
- Query methods support FilterSpec translation (reuse patterns from D1)

## Depends On
- E2 (domain types must exist)
- E1 implicitly (DB models must exist)
close_reason: All acceptance criteria met: ports defined, both SqlAlchemy repos implement all methods, atomic publish verified, tests pass with in-memory SQLite
